---
title: "Time-Stepping for ODEs and Stability"
description: "How ordinary differential equations become update rules on a time grid, why explicit and implicit methods behave differently on decaying dynamics, and why stability matters over many steps rather than only at one local approximation."
page-type: concept
status: published
update-status: current
level: advanced
topic: numerical-methods
prerequisites:
  - /topics/foundation/single-variable-calculus/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/concepts/approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Explain why solving an ODE numerically means choosing a time-stepping rule on a grid."
  - "Recognize the difference between local truncation error and long-time stability."
  - "Understand the basic contrast between explicit Euler and implicit Euler."
  - "Explain stiffness as a situation where stability, not only accuracy, controls step-size choice."
applications:
  - scientific-computing
  - engineering-systems
  - control-and-dynamics
keywords:
  - ODE
  - time stepping
  - Euler method
  - stability
  - stiffness
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: annual
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
related-pages:
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/index.qmd
  - /topics/foundation/single-variable-calculus/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd
  - /topics/advanced/numerical-methods/concepts/approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd
stable-sources:
  - "MIT 18.335J Introduction to Numerical Methods"
  - "MIT 18.086 difference methods for ODEs"
  - "Stanford CME108 scientific computing"
current-sources:
  - "MIT 18.335J resource index"
  - "MIT 18.086 lecture 1 difference methods for ODEs"
  - "MIT 2.086 stiff equations lecture"
  - "Stanford CME108 syllabus pdf"
  - "Stanford CS137 syllabus"
  - "Stanford CME108 bulletin"
---

## Role

This is an optional extension page in the `Numerical Methods` module.

Its job is to show how the approximation viewpoint from the previous page turns into actual time-marching algorithms for differential equations, and why repeated stepping introduces a new concern:

`a method can look locally reasonable and still behave badly over many steps`

## First-Pass Promise

Read this page after [Approximation, Differentiation, Integration, and Error Control](approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd).

If you stop here, you should still understand:

- why numerical ODE solving means replacing continuous time by discrete steps
- why local truncation error is not the whole story
- how explicit and implicit Euler differ
- why stiffness makes stability dominate step-size choice

## Why It Matters

Many continuous models are written as ODEs:

- mechanical systems
- circuits and controls
- chemical kinetics
- population models
- gradient-flow and continuous-time optimization viewpoints

To compute with them, we replace the exact trajectory by values on a time grid:

$$
t_0,\ t_1,\ t_2,\dots
$$

and build update rules that move from one time level to the next.

That creates a new type of numerical question.

For linear solves or quadrature, we usually focus on the quality of one approximation.

For ODEs, we must also ask:

- what happens after many repeated steps?
- does the method amplify harmless numerical noise?
- does the method respect decay that the true solution should have?

That is why stability is central.

## Prerequisite Recall

- finite-difference ideas replace derivatives by local formulas
- truncation error measures how much a finite formula misses the exact continuous object
- adaptive error control balances computational cost against approximation quality

## Intuition

### Time Stepping As Repeated Approximation

For an ODE

$$
y'(t)=f(t,y(t)),
$$

a one-step method turns the derivative information into an update from `y_n` to `y_{n+1}`.

So the method is not only approximating one derivative. It is composing the same approximation over and over.

### Explicit Euler

The simplest idea uses the slope at the current point:

$$
y_{n+1}=y_n+h f(t_n,y_n).
$$

This is cheap and intuitive, but stability can force the step size `h` to be small.

### Implicit Euler

Another idea uses the slope at the next time level:

$$
y_{n+1}=y_n+h f(t_{n+1},y_{n+1}).
$$

Now each step is more expensive because we must solve an equation for `y_{n+1}`, but the method is often much more stable.

### Stiffness

Some ODEs contain rapidly decaying modes that do not matter much to the long-term shape of the solution, but they still force explicit methods to take tiny steps for stability.

That is the first-pass intuition for stiffness:

`the dynamics are easy to describe qualitatively, but hard to step through explicitly without very small h`

## Formal Core

::: {#def-onestep}
## Definition: One-Step Time-Stepping Method

A one-step method updates the approximate solution by a rule of the form

$$
y_{n+1}=\Phi_h(t_n,y_n).
$$
:::

The step size `h` controls both the cost and the approximation quality.

::: {#def-forward-euler}
## Definition: Explicit Euler

For

$$
y'(t)=f(t,y(t)),
$$

explicit Euler uses

$$
y_{n+1}=y_n+h f(t_n,y_n).
$$
:::

::: {#def-backward-euler}
## Definition: Implicit Euler

Implicit Euler uses

$$
y_{n+1}=y_n+h f(t_{n+1},y_{n+1}).
$$
:::

This usually requires solving a nonlinear or linear equation at each step.

::: {#def-lte}
## Definition: Local Truncation Error

The local truncation error measures the one-step defect obtained by inserting the exact solution into the numerical update rule.
:::

This is the local accuracy story, but repeated stepping means we also need a stability story.

::: {#thm-test-eqn}
## Theorem Idea: Stability Is Read Through The Test Equation

A standard first-pass stability lens studies the scalar test equation

$$
y'=\lambda y.
$$

Applying a method gives an update

$$
y_{n+1}=R(h\lambda)\,y_n,
$$

where `R` is the method's stability function.
:::

The method is stable for that step size when repeated multiplication by `R(h\lambda)` does not create the wrong long-time behavior.

For explicit Euler,

$$
R(z)=1+z.
$$

For implicit Euler,

$$
R(z)=\frac{1}{1-z}.
$$

## A Small Worked Example

Consider the decaying ODE

$$
y'=-10y,
\qquad
y(0)=1.
$$

The exact solution is

$$
y(t)=e^{-10t},
$$

so the true behavior is monotone decay.

### Explicit Euler

Explicit Euler gives

$$
y_{n+1}=(1-10h)y_n.
$$

To keep the magnitude from growing, we need

$$
|1-10h|<1,
$$

which means

$$
0<h<0.2.
$$

So even for a very simple decaying problem, stability already restricts the step size.

### Implicit Euler

Implicit Euler gives

$$
y_{n+1}=\frac{1}{1+10h}y_n.
$$

For every `h>0`, this factor has magnitude less than `1`, so the method keeps the decaying behavior for any positive step size.

This example is the first-pass stability lesson:

- explicit Euler is cheap but conditionally stable
- implicit Euler is more expensive per step but much more stable for decaying modes

## Computation Lens

When you face a time-stepping problem, ask:

1. is the main bottleneck accuracy or stability?
2. if I halve the step size, do I gain real accuracy or am I only paying a stability tax?
3. does the method need to preserve decay, boundedness, or some qualitative structure of the true solution?
4. is the equation stiff enough that an implicit method is the more honest computational model?

Those questions usually matter more than memorizing a list of named schemes.

## Application Lens

### Simulation And Engineering

Physical simulation often depends on whether the method tracks the right long-time behavior, not only whether one step is locally accurate.

### Control And Dynamics

State evolution, feedback models, and discretized continuous-time systems all rely on time stepping and stability language.

### Optimization And Continuous-Time Views

Gradient flows and dynamical-system interpretations of optimization become computational only after choosing a time discretization, and different discretizations behave differently.

## Stop Here For First Pass

If you can now explain:

- why ODE solvers are repeated local approximations
- why stability is a separate concern from local truncation error
- why explicit and implicit Euler behave differently on decaying dynamics
- why stiffness is the reason some problems force tiny explicit time steps

then this page has done its job.

## Go Deeper

The strongest adjacent pages are:

- [Numerical Methods](../index.qmd)
- [ODEs and Dynamical Systems](../../odes-and-dynamical-systems/index.qmd)
- [Discretization, Time-Stepping, and the Bridge to Control](../../odes-and-dynamical-systems/concepts/discretization-time-stepping-and-the-bridge-to-control.qmd)
- [Approximation, Differentiation, Integration, and Error Control](approximation-differentiation-integration-and-error-control.qmd)
- [Optimization](../../optimization/index.qmd)
- [Multivariable Calculus](../../../foundation/multivariable-calculus/index.qmd)

## Optional Deeper Reading After First Pass

The strongest current references connected to this page are:

- [MIT 18.335J resource index](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-335j-introduction-to-numerical-methods-spring-2019/pages/resource-index) - official MIT course resource map showing numerical methods for ODEs as a central lecture block. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.086 lecture 1 difference methods for ODEs](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-086-mathematical-methods-for-engineers-ii-spring-2006/resources/lecture-1-difference-methods-for-ordinary-differential-equations/) - official MIT lecture resource for the basic difference-method and stability viewpoint. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 2.086 stiff equations lecture](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/2-086-numerical-computation-for-mechanical-engineers-spring-2013/resources/mit2_086s13_lecture16/) - official MIT lecture page focused on stiff ODEs. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CME108 syllabus pdf](https://cme108.stanford.edu/syllabus.pdf) - official syllabus showing ODE time stepping as a natural continuation of the approximation toolkit. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CS137 syllabus](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs137/syllabus.html) - official current scientific-computing syllabus linking quadrature and ODE methods in one numerical arc. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CME108 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1056641) - official course description placing ODEs inside the broader scientific-computing curriculum. Checked `2026-04-25`.

## Sources and Further Reading

- [MIT 18.335J resource index](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-335j-introduction-to-numerical-methods-spring-2019/pages/resource-index) - `First pass` - official MIT course map for the ODE-methods block. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 18.086 lecture 1 difference methods for ODEs](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/18-086-mathematical-methods-for-engineers-ii-spring-2006/resources/lecture-1-difference-methods-for-ordinary-differential-equations/) - `First pass` - official MIT lecture resource for difference methods and stability ideas. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [MIT 2.086 stiff equations lecture](https://ocw.mit.edu/courses/2-086-numerical-computation-for-mechanical-engineers-spring-2013/resources/mit2_086s13_lecture16/) - `First pass` - official MIT lecture page focused on stiff ODE intuition. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CME108 syllabus pdf](https://cme108.stanford.edu/syllabus.pdf) - `Second pass` - official syllabus placing time stepping inside a larger scientific-computing toolkit. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CS137 syllabus](https://web.stanford.edu/class/cs137/syllabus.html) - `Second pass` - official current syllabus linking numerical integration and ODE methods. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Stanford CME108 bulletin](https://bulletin.stanford.edu/courses/1056641) - `Second pass` - official course description connecting ODE methods to the broader scientific-computing curriculum. Checked `2026-04-25`.
